metadata.summary: Decision tree for selecting technology stack — frontend frameworks, backend languages, and mobile platforms — based on application type, SEO needs, and team expertise.

content.title: Technology Stack Selection Decision Tree
content.summary: Walk this decision tree to select frontend frameworks, backend languages, and mobile platforms. The tree routes by application type first, then SEO requirements and team expertise. Output: recommended stack, runner-up, and rationale for the choice.

why.p: Technology selection is the second most impactful architecture decision after style choice. Poor technology fit forces rewrites, raises hiring costs, and compounds technical debt. A decision tree makes the selection reproducible and documents the reasoning for future reviewers.

Team expertise is consistently the most heavily weighted factor (15-30%) in real-world selections because retraining cost is a real, measurable expense. The tree encodes 2025 market share data so recommendations stay grounded in ecosystem reality rather than preference.

list.item: Starting a new project or service and need to pick a technology stack.
Evaluating whether to adopt a new framework for an existing codebase.
Onboarding a team that needs a defensible technology choice with documented rationale.
Filtering 5-10 candidate technologies down to 2-3 for a deeper comparison matrix.
When a hard constraint already gates the choice (contractual, compliance, existing vendor) — the constraint dominates the tree output.
For low-stakes tooling choices (CLI scripts, one-off automation) — pick what the team knows and move on.
When the team has deep expertise in one option and no strong counter-indicator — expertise weight will dominate anyway.

section.name: technology-stack-tree
section.p: Walk by application type first. Each branch then routes on secondary factors (SEO, performance tier, team expertise).

rule.statement: When team expertise exists for an option, it MUST receive a weight of at least 25% in the decision matrix. If the team-preferred option scores within 0.5 points of the top-scoring option, prefer the team-familiar option and document the trade-off.
rule.rationale: Retraining cost and hiring friction are real costs that decision matrices undercount. A 0.5-point performance advantage does not justify 3-6 months of team ramp-up time on a new stack.

example.title: Full technology stack decision tree
example.code: Q1: What type of application? | +-- "Web Application" ---------------------------------------+ | Q2: SEO requirements? | | | | | +-- "Critical (marketing, e-commerce)" --------------+ | | -> Next.js or Astro (SSR/SSG) | | | | | +-- "Not critical (dashboard, internal tool)" -------+ | Q3: Team expertise? | | +-- "React" -> React + Vite | | +-- "Vue" -> Vue + Nuxt | | +-- "Angular" -> Angular | | +-- "None/New" -> Next.js (largest ecosystem)| | | +-- "API/Backend" -------------------------------------------+ | Q2: Performance requirements? | | | | | +-- "Standard (<1000 RPS)" --------------------------+ | | Q3: Team expertise? | | | +-- "Python" -> Django or FastAPI | | | +-- "JavaScript" -> Node.js + Express/Fastify| | | +-- "Java/.NET" -> Spring Boot or .NET | | | +-- "None" -> FastAPI (Python) or Go | | | | | +-- "High performance (>10k RPS)" -------------------+ | -> Go, Rust, or C++ | | | +-- "Mobile Application" ------------------------------------+ | Q2: Platform requirements? | | +-- "Both iOS + Android" -> React Native or Flutter | | +-- "iOS only" -> Swift/SwiftUI | | +-- "Android only" -> Kotlin | | +-- "Web + Mobile" -> React Native + Next.js | | | +-- "Data/ML Pipeline" --------------------------------------+ -> Python (pandas, Spark, dbt, Airflow)

section.name: market-share-2025
section.p: Framework market share data (2025) for anchoring decisions:

example.title: Framework market share 2025
example.code: Category | Leader | Usage ----------------|--------------|--------------------------- Frontend | React | 39% Meta-framework | Next.js | 68% of React devs Backend (Python)| FastAPI | Growing fastest Backend (JS) | Express | 50%+ Mobile | React Native | 42% cross-platform
example.note: Refresh these numbers annually — LLMs anchor on stale data. Use WebSearch to verify current market share before finalizing a selection.

section.name: frontend-example
section.p: Real-world example: InsightBoard — data visualization dashboard, complex charts + real-time updates + SEO for marketing pages, team of 4 frontend developers (3 React, 1 Vue).

example.title: InsightBoard: Next.js selection
example.code:
Q1: SEO requirements? -> "Critical for marketing pages, not for app" Implication: Need SSR/SSG for marketing, SPA acceptable for app
Q2: Team expertise? -> "3 React devs, 1 Vue dev"
Q3: Complexity of UI? -> "High - complex charts, drag-drop, real-time"
Options: Next.js, Remix, Nuxt, SvelteKit
Decision matrix (weighted): Criteria | Weight | Next.js | Remix | Nuxt | SvelteKit Team expertise | 30% | 5 | 4 | 2 | 1 Ecosystem | 25% | 5 | 3 | 4 | 3 Performance | 20% | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 DX | 15% | 4 | 5 | 5 | 5 Hiring pool | 10% | 5 | 3 | 4 | 2 Weighted Score | 100% | 4.4 | 3.7 | 3.2 | 2.9
Chosen: Next.js 14 (App Router) Architecture: / -> Marketing (SSG) /pricing -> Marketing (SSG) /blog/* -> Blog (SSG) /app/* -> Dashboard (RSC + Client Components) /api/* -> API routes (tRPC)
example.note: 75% of team already proficient in React. WebSocket via Socket.io for real-time charts. Recharts for visualization.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.p: Prompts for LLM-assisted technology stack selection:
section.prompt:
Help me evaluate technology options for <specific need>.
Context: - Project type: <Web app, API, mobile, etc.> - Team expertise: <List current skills> - Performance requirements: <Latency, throughput targets> - Scale expectations: <Users, data volume, growth> - Budget: <Cost constraints> - Timeline: <Deadline>
Options I'm considering: 1. <Option 1> 2. <Option 2> 3. <Option 3>
Please provide: 1. Comparison table with key criteria 2. Decision matrix with weighted scores 3. Your recommendation with rationale 4. Hidden gotchas or considerations I might miss 5. Learning resources for the recommended option

I need to choose a <frontend/backend/mobile> framework.
Requirements: - Type: <SPA, SSR, API, etc.> - SEO importance: <Critical/Not important> - Real-time features: <Yes/No, describe> - Integrations needed: <List key integrations> - Team background: <Current skills and experience> - Performance targets: <Specific metrics>
Constraints: - Must support: <Specific features> - Cannot use: <Any excluded options> - Budget for tools/hosting: <Amount>
Please create a decision tree and recommend the best framework with detailed justification.

We're building a new service and need to choose a programming language.
Service Purpose: <What the service does>
Requirements: - Performance: <Latency/throughput needs> - Concurrency: <Number of concurrent operations> - Team skills: <Current language expertise> - Ecosystem needs: <Required libraries, integrations> - Deployment: <Container, serverless, VM>
Current Stack: <What we already use>
Please provide: 1. Top 3 language recommendations with pros/cons 2. Performance comparison for our use case 3. Ecosystem assessment (libraries, tools) 4. Team ramp-up time estimates 5. Long-term maintainability considerations

antipattern.description: Agent recommends a technology purely on benchmark scores, ignoring that the team has zero experience with it.
antipattern.reason: Team expertise must be weighted at 25%+ in the matrix. A 10% performance advantage does not justify 3-6 months of ramp-up on an unfamiliar stack.

antipattern.description: Agent outputs a single recommendation without the runner-up and its key trade-off.
antipattern.reason: Forcing the runner-up reveals the most fragile input and what would flip the decision — essential context for the architect.

antipattern.description: Agent uses stale 2023 market share data (e.g., Angular at 30% usage) without refreshing.
antipattern.reason: Technology landscapes shift fast; citations older than 12 months must be verified via WebSearch before anchoring a recommendation.
